***Fringe*** is an American [science fiction television](/wiki/Science_fiction_television "Science fiction television") series created by [J. J. Abrams](/wiki/J._J._Abrams "J. J. Abrams"), [Alex Kurtzman](/wiki/Alex_Kurtzman "Alex Kurtzman"), and [Roberto Orci](/wiki/Roberto_Orci "Roberto Orci"). It premiered on [Fox](/wiki/Fox_Broadcasting_Company "Fox Broadcasting Company") on September 9, 2008, and concluded on January 18, 2013, after [five seasons comprising 100 episodes](/wiki/List_of_Fringe_episodes "List of Fringe episodes"). An FBI agent, [Olivia Dunham](/wiki/Olivia_Dunham "Olivia Dunham") ([Anna Torv](/wiki/Anna_Torv "Anna Torv")), a genius but dysfunctional scientist, [Walter Bishop](/wiki/Walter_Bishop_(Fringe) "Walter Bishop (Fringe)") ([John Noble](/wiki/John_Noble "John Noble")), and his son with a troubled past, [Peter Bishop](/wiki/Peter_Bishop "Peter Bishop") ([Joshua Jackson](/wiki/Joshua_Jackson "Joshua Jackson")), are all members of a newly formed Fringe Division in the [Federal Bureau of Investigation](/wiki/Federal_Bureau_of_Investigation "Federal Bureau of Investigation"). Based in [Boston](/wiki/Boston "Boston"), [Massachusetts](/wiki/Massachusetts "Massachusetts"), the team uses [fringe science](/wiki/Fringe_science "Fringe science") to investigate a series of unexplained and often ghastly occurrences which are related to a [parallel universe](/wiki/Parallel_universes_in_fiction "Parallel universes in fiction").[[1]](#cite_note-1)

The series has been described as a hybrid of fantasy, [procedural dramas](/wiki/Procedural_drama "Procedural drama"), and [serials](/wiki/Serial_(radio_and_television) "Serial (radio and television)"), influenced by films like *[Altered States](/wiki/Altered_States "Altered States")* and television shows such as *[Lost](/wiki/Lost_(TV_series) "Lost (TV series)")*, *[The X-Files](/wiki/The_X-Files "The X-Files")*, and *[The Twilight Zone](/wiki/The_Twilight_Zone_(1959_TV_series) "The Twilight Zone (1959 TV series)")*. The series began as a traditional [mystery-of-the-week](/wiki/Villain_of_the_week "Villain of the week") series and became more serialized in later seasons. Most episodes contain a standalone plot, with several others also exploring the series' overarching [mythology](/wiki/Mythology_of_Fringe "Mythology of Fringe").[[2]](#cite_note-2)

Critical reception was lukewarm at first but became more favorable after the first season, when the series began to explore its mythology, including parallel universes with [alternate timelines](/wiki/Alternate_history "Alternate history"). The show, along with cast and crew, was nominated for many major awards. Despite its move to the "[Friday night death slot](/wiki/Friday_night_death_slot "Friday night death slot")" and low [ratings](/wiki/Nielsen_ratings "Nielsen ratings"), the series developed a [cult following](/wiki/Cult_following "Cult following"). It also spawned two six-part [comic book](/wiki/Comic_book "Comic book") series, an [alternate reality game](/wiki/Alternate_reality_game "Alternate reality game"), and three novels.

## Premise

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Fringe_(TV_series)&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Premise")]

*Fringe* follows the casework of the Fringe Division, a Joint Federal Task Force supported primarily by the [Federal Bureau of Investigation](/wiki/Federal_Bureau_of_Investigation "Federal Bureau of Investigation"), which includes Agent [Olivia Dunham](/wiki/Olivia_Dunham "Olivia Dunham"), [Dr. Walter Bishop](/wiki/Dr._Walter_Bishop "Dr. Walter Bishop"), the archetypal [mad scientist](/wiki/Mad_scientist "Mad scientist"), and [Peter Bishop](/wiki/Peter_Bishop "Peter Bishop"), Walter's estranged son and jack-of-all-trades. They are supported by Phillip Broyles ([Lance Reddick](/wiki/Lance_Reddick "Lance Reddick")), the force's director, and Agent Astrid Farnsworth ([Jasika Nicole](/wiki/Jasika_Nicole "Jasika Nicole")), originally Dunham's assistant, who assists Walter in laboratory research. The Fringe Division investigates cases relating to [fringe science](/wiki/Fringe_science "Fringe science"), ranging from [transhumanist](/wiki/Transhumanist "Transhumanist") experiments gone wrong to the prospect of a destructive [technological singularity](/wiki/Technological_singularity "Technological singularity") to a possible collision of two [parallel universes](/wiki/Parallel_universes_in_fiction "Parallel universes in fiction"). The Fringe Division's work often intersects with advanced biotechnology developed by a company called [Massive Dynamic](/wiki/Massive_Dynamic "Massive Dynamic"), founded by Walter's former partner, Dr. William Bell ([Leonard Nimoy](/wiki/Leonard_Nimoy "Leonard Nimoy")), and run by their common friend, Nina Sharp ([Blair Brown](/wiki/Blair_Brown "Blair Brown")). The team is also watched silently by a group of bald, pale white men who are called "Observers".

## Overview

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Fringe_(TV_series)&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Overview")]

Main article: [List of Fringe episodes](/wiki/List_of_Fringe_episodes "List of Fringe episodes")

[Season 1](/wiki/Fringe_season_1 "Fringe season 1") introduces the Fringe Division as they investigate cases that form "the Pattern" geographically centered around Reiden Lake in New York state, many of which are orchestrated by an international network of rogue scientists known as ZFT (*Zerstörung durch Fortschritte der Technologie*, or in English, Destruction through Advancement of Technology), led by [David Robert Jones](/wiki/List_of_Fringe_characters#David_Robert_Jones "List of Fringe characters") ([Jared Harris](/wiki/Jared_Harris "Jared Harris")), who are preparing for a [doomsday event](/wiki/Doomsday_event "Doomsday event"). The ZFT threat appears to end when Peter kills Jones as he attempts travel to a parallel universe.[[3]](#cite_note-season_1_finale-3) Olivia comes to learn she was a child test subject for Walter years ago (then known as Olive) for a [nootropic](/wiki/Nootropic "Nootropic") drug, Cortexiphan, giving her weak [psionic](/wiki/Psionic "Psionic") abilities.[[4]](#cite_note-4) Walter also struggles with adjusting to normal life in Peter's care after living seventeen years in a mental institution.[[3]](#cite_note-season_1_finale-3)

In [Season 2](/wiki/Fringe_season_2 "Fringe season 2"), the occurrences are found to be in conjunction with activities of a parallel universe, which is plagued by [singularities](/wiki/Gravitational_singularity "Gravitational singularity") occurring at weakened points of the fabric between worlds; over there, scientists have developed an [amber](/wiki/Amber "Amber")-like substance that isolates these singularities as well as any innocent people caught in the area on its release. The Fringe team deals with more cases that are leading to a "great storm" as the parallel universe appears to be at war with the prime one, engineered by human-machine hybrid [shapeshifters](/wiki/Shapeshifters "Shapeshifters") from the parallel universe.[[5]](#cite_note-5) Walter is forced to tell Peter that he is from the parallel universe, a replacement for his own Peter, who died from a genetic disease. Walter had crossed over on the frozen ice of Reiden Lake in 1985 to administer the cure for the alternate version of Peter, but, after accidentally destroying a dose of the cure upon transport, he instead brought the boy across. On return, they fell through the ice but were saved by the Observer September ([Michael Cerveris](/wiki/Michael_Cerveris "Michael Cerveris")), who told Walter of the importance of "the boy", which Walter took to mean Peter.[[6]](#cite_note-6) Walter's crossing is what caused the singularities in the parallel universe, with Reiden Lake at their center. Walter has been looking for a sign of forgiveness in the form of a white tulip.[[7]](#cite_note-ap_fringe_summary-7)

[Season 3](/wiki/Fringe_season_3 "Fringe season 3") presents episodes that alternate between the two universes. "Walternate", Walter's [doppelgänger](/wiki/Doppelg%C3%A4nger "Doppelgänger") in the parallel universe, is the [U.S. Secretary of Defense](/wiki/U.S._Secretary_of_Defense "U.S. Secretary of Defense") and has set events in motion to assemble the Machine, a [doomsday device](/wiki/Doomsday_device "Doomsday device") that reacts only to Peter's biology.[[8]](#cite_note-8) He also sent his Olivia, "Fauxlivia"[2](#endnote_2), to the prime universe, in Olivia's place, to engage the Fringe Division and assemble the prime universe's version of the device, while he studies Olivia's Cortexiphan-induced powers.[[9]](#cite_note-9) By happenstance, Fauxlivia becomes pregnant with Peter's child, Henry, before being outed and extracted to the parallel universe.[[10]](#cite_note-10) Walternate orchestrated acceleration of the pregnancy to gain a sample of the baby's blood, which he uses to activate the machine.[[11]](#cite_note-11) Peter, with Olivia's help, enters the prime version of the machine, and experiences a vision of the future where the parallel universe has been destroyed and the same fate threatens the prime one, and learns the Machine is really a device created by Walter and his associates from this future, sent back in time purposely to relay this vision of the future to Peter. Recovering in the present, Peter alters his plan and uses the Machine to merge the two rooms, creating a [bridge](/wiki/Einstein-Rosen_bridge "Einstein-Rosen bridge") where inhabitants of both universes can solve their dilemma, before time is re-written so September (The Observer) does not save him and is forgotten by both Walter and Olivia.[[12]](#cite_note-12)

[Season 4](/wiki/Fringe_season_4 "Fringe season 4") begins in an [alternate timeline](/wiki/Alternate_timeline "Alternate timeline"), one in which September had failed to save the alternate version of Peter in 1985, according to the Observers.[[13]](#cite_note-13)[[14]](#cite_note-14) This creates a [butterfly effect](/wiki/Butterfly_effect "Butterfly effect") influencing the main characters' pasts but otherwise stabilizing both universes due to the creation of the bridge. Peter is pulled into this new timeline due to the actions of the alternate timeline's Fringe team, which includes [Lincoln Lee](/wiki/Lincoln_Lee "Lincoln Lee") ([Seth Gabel](/wiki/Seth_Gabel "Seth Gabel")). Peter initially works to return to his own timeline, fueled by fears that his memories are altering Cortexiphan-dosed Olivia's of this timeline, but after encountering a wounded September, Peter comes to learn that this timeline is truly his home, and both he and Olivia come to accept the change, rekindling their affair.[[15]](#cite_note-15) September also reveals to Peter that the Observers needed to erase Peter's son, Henry, to assure their future will be created, though noting that Peter's future child with Olivia will be important.[[16]](#cite_note-16) Meanwhile, in the present, William Bell has instructed David Robert Jones, alive in this timeline, to work with the parallel universe's version of Nina Sharp to synchronize the two universes, aiming to collapse them both and pave the way for a third universe under Bell's control, using Olivia's Cortexiphan powers to enable the collapse. The Fringe division is forced to close the dimensional bridge, but this fails to stop Bell's plan. Walter is left with one choice, to shoot and kill Olivia, her death disrupting the process and saving the world. Olivia's "death" is only temporary, as the Cortexiphan in her body is consumed to repair the bullet wound, leaving her alive and healthy but lacking her psionic abilities. As Olivia and Peter begin their lives together, September appears to Walter and warns that the Observers "are coming".[[17]](#cite_note-17)

The [fifth and final season](/wiki/Fringe_season_5 "Fringe season 5") begins in 2036, following from the flash-forward fourth-season episode "Letters of Transit".[[18]](#cite_note-18) As September warned, Observers, bald white males from the far future, having ruined Earth for themselves, time-traveled to 2015 and instituted "The Purge", wiped out much of humanity, subjected the survivors to their control, and began modifying the planet's environment to be more suitable for themselves.[[19]](#cite_note-19) The Fringe team was able to seal themselves in amber to avoid capture shortly after the Purge, and are reunited through Henrietta ("Etta") ([Georgina Haig](/wiki/Georgina_Haig "Georgina Haig")), Peter and Olivia's now adult daughter who disappeared shortly after the Observer arrival in 2015. Walter reveals he and September developed a plan to defeat the Observers, revealed through a series of pre-recorded videotapes ambered in the lab. The tapes lead to several components of a device, including a young Observer child, named Michael ([Spencer List in Season 1](/wiki/Spencer_List "Spencer List") and Rowan Longworth in Season 5), but further allude to a man named Donald that had helped Walter prepare the plan. Etta is killed during these events, driving Olivia and Peter to complete the plan for her sake. Through Michael, they discover Donald is September, having been stripped of his Observer powers for helping the Fringe team, and that Michael is his genetic son, having been purposely grown as an anomaly in the far future. September explains the plan is to send Michael to the year 2167, where human genetic experiments to sacrifice emotion for intelligence would be started and leading to the creation of the Observers; by showing them Michael, who possesses both emotion and intelligence, the experiments would be stopped and the Observers never created. September is prepared to take Michael to the future as the plan is set in motion, but he is shot and killed at the last moment; Walter, already made aware that he will have to make a sacrifice, takes Michael through to the future to assure the plan's completion. As predicted, time is reset from the invasion onwards in 2015; the Observers never invade, and Peter, Olivia, and Etta live their lives peacefully—though Peter receives one final letter from his father: a drawing of a white tulip.[[20]](#cite_note-20)

## Mythology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Fringe_(TV_series)&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Mythology")]

Main article: [Mythology of Fringe](/wiki/Mythology_of_Fringe "Mythology of Fringe")

### Parallel universe

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Fringe_(TV_series)&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Parallel universe")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/f/fb/Fringe-Manhatan.jpg/250px-Fringe-Manhatan.jpg)](/wiki/File:Fringe-Manhatan.jpg)

One of *Fringe*'s location titles. In this example, from "[Olivia](/wiki/Olivia_(Fringe_episode) "Olivia (Fringe episode)")", the episode takes place in the alternate universe's version of [Manhattan](/wiki/Manhattan "Manhattan"), whose name is spelled with only one T. One notable difference in the alternate universe is that while the [September 11 attacks](/wiki/September_11_attacks "September 11 attacks") occurred, the [World Trade Center](/wiki/World_Trade_Center_(1973%E2%80%932001) "World Trade Center (1973–2001)") towers were not destroyed, but the [White House](/wiki/White_House "White House") was.

Much of the [story arc](/wiki/Story_arc "Story arc") for *Fringe* involves a [parallel universe](/wiki/Parallel_universes_in_fiction "Parallel universes in fiction") that mostly mirrors the prime universe, but with numerous historical idiosyncrasies. A significant example element used is the effect of the [September 11 attacks](/wiki/September_11_attacks "September 11 attacks"); though this event also occurred in the alternate universe, the [World Trade Center](/wiki/World_Trade_Center_(1973%E2%80%932001) "World Trade Center (1973–2001)") was untouched by the attacks, leaving the buildings as predominant landmarks in the alternate world's skyline of "Manhatan". The South Tower was used as the office of William Bell in several episodes.[[21]](#cite_note-nypost_over_there-21)[[22]](#cite_note-22)

The producers were strongly interested in "[world-building](/wiki/World-building "World-building")", and the alternate universe allowed them to create a very similar world with a large amount of detail to fill in the texture of the world. An alternate universe also allowed them to show "how small choices that you make define you as a person and can change your life in large ways down the line," according to co-director [Jeff Pinkner](/wiki/Jeff_Pinkner "Jeff Pinkner").[[21]](#cite_note-nypost_over_there-21) However, the producers also realized the concept of the alternate universe could confuse viewers. To avoid this, they introduced elements of the world in small pieces over the course of the first two seasons before the larger revelation in the second-season finale and the third season. [J. H. Wyman](/wiki/J._H._Wyman "J. H. Wyman") stated that he would often pass the story ideas for the alternate universe by his father to see if it made sense, and would rework the script if his father found it confusing.[[23]](#cite_note-usatoday_parallel_universe-23) Such world building also gave them a risky opportunity to create stories that focused solely on characters from the alternate universe with nearly no ties to the main characters; as stated by Wyman, they would be able to "make two shows about one show," a concept that the network executives embraced.[[24]](#cite_note-ign_march2011_part2-24)

### Glyph code

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Fringe_(TV_series)&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Glyph code")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/b/be/Fringe_leaf.jpg/250px-Fringe_leaf.jpg)](/wiki/File:Fringe_leaf.jpg)

Marketing posters used to promote the series often featured one of the glyphs used in the show's interstitial, each being a twist on a common image. Pictured here is a leaf with an embedded Greek capital letter delta.

Prior to commercial breaks, a brief image of a [glyph](/wiki/Glyph "Glyph") is shown. Abrams revealed in an interview that the glyphs had a hidden meaning. "It's something that we're doing for people who care to figure it out and follow it, but it's not something that a viewer has to consider when they watch the show."[[25]](#cite_note-mystery-25) Abrams also revealed that the seemingly unrelated frogs which have the [Greek letter](/wiki/Greek_letter "Greek letter") [Phi](/wiki/Phi "Phi") (Φ) imprinted on their backs appeared in promos for the show and have significance within the context of the series, saying "it's part of the code of the show."[[25]](#cite_note-mystery-25) The glyph code was cracked by [Julian Sanchez](/wiki/Julian_Sanchez_(writer) "Julian Sanchez (writer)"), then working as an editor at the technology site *[Ars Technica](/wiki/Ars_Technica "Ars Technica")*, who discovered it to be a simple [substitution cipher](/wiki/Substitution_cipher "Substitution cipher") used to spell out a single thematic word for each episode; for example, the pilot episode's eight glyphs spell out the word "observer".[[26]](#cite_note-cracked-26) Additionally, the glyphs are representative of some of the means by which Walter solves a case (such as the moth/butterfly from "[Johari Window](/wiki/Johari_Window_(Fringe) "Johari Window (Fringe)")", the seahorse strain of DNA from "[The Bishop Revival](/wiki/The_Bishop_Revival "The Bishop Revival")"). In "[Jacksonville](/wiki/Jacksonville_(Fringe) "Jacksonville (Fringe)")", behind Walter as he speaks to Olivia about her treatment where the [nootropic](/wiki/Nootropic "Nootropic") Cortexiphan was first studied as a trial, each of the glyphs is clearly visible on the daycare wall. An episode-by-episode key to the various glyphs was made available on Fringepedia.[[27]](#cite_note-27)

### Opening sequence

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Fringe_(TV_series)&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Opening sequence")]

The show's standard opening sequence interplays images of the glyph symbols alongside words representing fringe science topics, such as "teleportation" and "dark matter." Within the third season, with episodes that took place primarily in the parallel universe, a new set of titles was used, following a similar format, though tinted red instead of blue and using alternate fringe science concepts like "hypnosis" and "neuroscience". The difference in color has led some fans to call the prime universe the Blue one in contrast to the parallel Red one.[[28]](#cite_note-28) In the third-season episode "[Entrada](/wiki/Entrada_(Fringe) "Entrada (Fringe)")", the titles used a mix of both the blue- and red-tinted versions, given that the episode took place equally in each universe.[[29]](#cite_note-29) In the two flashback episodes, "[Peter](/wiki/Peter_(Fringe_episode) "Peter (Fringe episode)")" and "[Subject 13](/wiki/Subject_13_(Fringe) "Subject 13 (Fringe)")," a variation on the sequence, with retro graphics akin to 1980s technology and phrases like "personal computing" and "genetic engineering" was used.[[30]](#cite_note-30) For the [dystopian](/wiki/Dystopian "Dystopian") future third-season episode "[The Day We Died](/wiki/The_Day_We_Died "The Day We Died")," a black-toned theme with more dire phrases like "hope" and "water" was used.[[31]](#cite_note-31) The fourth-season premiere, "[Neither Here Nor There](/wiki/Neither_Here_Nor_There_(Fringe) "Neither Here Nor There (Fringe)")", introduced an amber-toned title sequence, with additional new terms, that is used for nearly all fourth-season episodes.[[32]](#cite_note-32) The fourth-season episode "[Letters of Transit](/wiki/Letters_of_Transit "Letters of Transit")," which returned to the future dystopian universe, and the subsequent fifth-season episodes, feature a cold-toned title sequence with phrases such as "joy," "private thought," "free will," and "freedom," ideas which have been lost in this future.[[33]](#cite_note-33) There is one frame in the opening sequence in which the words "Observers are here" flash very quickly, and the opening sequence must be paused to see them.[[34]](#cite_note-34)

### Main

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Fringe_(TV_series)&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Main")]

* [Anna Torv](/wiki/Anna_Torv "Anna Torv") as [Olivia Dunham](/wiki/Olivia_Dunham "Olivia Dunham"): a [Northwestern University](/wiki/Northwestern_University "Northwestern University")-educated [Federal Bureau of Investigation](/wiki/Federal_Bureau_of_Investigation "Federal Bureau of Investigation") (FBI) [agent](/wiki/Special_agent "Special agent") assigned to investigate the spread of unexplained phenomena. She later discovers Walter performed tests on her when she was a child using [nootropic](/wiki/Nootropic "Nootropic") drug Cortexiphan, giving her unusual abilities. Torv also plays Olivia's counterpart in the [parallel universe](/wiki/Parallel_universes_in_fiction "Parallel universes in fiction"), dubbed by the characters of the prime universe as "Fauxlivia", as well as William Bell.
* [Joshua Jackson](/wiki/Joshua_Jackson "Joshua Jackson") as [Peter Bishop](/wiki/Peter_Bishop "Peter Bishop"): a [jack of all trades](/wiki/Jack_of_all_trades "Jack of all trades") who is brought in as a civilian consultant by Olivia to work with his estranged father, Walter. Peter is actually "Walternate's" son from the alternate universe, abducted by Walter shortly after his own Peter's death at a young age.
* [John Noble](/wiki/John_Noble "John Noble") as [Dr. Walter Bishop](/wiki/Dr._Walter_Bishop "Dr. Walter Bishop"): a former government researcher in the field of [fringe science](/wiki/Fringe_science "Fringe science") who was seen as a [mad scientist](/wiki/Mad_scientist "Mad scientist") and institutionalized after a lab accident in which his assistant was killed. Noble also portrays the Walter's alternate, dubbed "Walternate" by the characters in the prime universe. Walternate rose to power as the [U.S. Secretary of Defense](/wiki/U.S._Secretary_of_Defense "U.S. Secretary of Defense") and instigated the war against the prime universe after the abduction of his son Peter.
* [Lance Reddick](/wiki/Lance_Reddick "Lance Reddick") as [Phillip Broyles](/wiki/Phillip_Broyles "Phillip Broyles") (seasons 1–4; recurring season 5): a [Homeland Security](/wiki/United_States_Department_of_Homeland_Security "United States Department of Homeland Security") agent and Senior-Agent-in-Charge (SAC) who runs Fringe Division. Reddick also portrays Broyles' alternate (known as Colonel Broyles), who finds sympathy for Olivia and sacrifices himself during the third season to allow her to escape the parallel universe. In the fourth season's alternate timeline, Colonel Broyles remains alive.
* [Kirk Acevedo](/wiki/Kirk_Acevedo "Kirk Acevedo") as [Charlie Francis](/wiki/Charlie_Francis_(Fringe) "Charlie Francis (Fringe)") (seasons 1–2; recurring season 3): An FBI senior agent, Olivia's colleague and close friend, and the second-in-command of Fringe Division before his demise. Though Charlie is killed early in the second season, Acevedo reprises the role of Charlie in the [alternate universe](/wiki/Parallel_universe_(fiction) "Parallel universe (fiction)").
* [Blair Brown](/wiki/Blair_Brown "Blair Brown") as [Nina Sharp](/wiki/Nina_Sharp "Nina Sharp") (seasons 1–4; recurring season 5): the Chief Operating Officer of Massive Dynamic, a leading firm in science and technology research and longtime friend of Walter and William. She also plays her doppelganger in the parallel universe within the alternate timeline of season 4 as an agent for David Robert Jones' plans.
* [Jasika Nicole](/wiki/Jasika_Nicole "Jasika Nicole") as [Astrid Farnsworth](/wiki/Astrid_Farnsworth "Astrid Farnsworth"): an FBI Junior Agent and assistant to Olivia and Walter. Astrid's counterpart in the alternate universe, who has behaviors similar to [autism](/wiki/Autism "Autism"), is also played by Nicole.[[35]](#cite_note-35)
* [Mark Valley](/wiki/Mark_Valley "Mark Valley") as [John Scott](/wiki/John_Scott_(Fringe) "John Scott (Fringe)") (season 1): Olivia's former FBI partner and secret lover, whose death in "Pilot" leads Olivia to join the Fringe division.
* [Seth Gabel](/wiki/Seth_Gabel "Seth Gabel") as [Lincoln Lee](/wiki/Lincoln_Lee "Lincoln Lee") (season 4; guest star seasons 2 and 5; recurring season 3): an agent of the parallel universe Fringe Division. The prime universe version of Lincoln, also played by Gabel, was introduced in the episode "[Stowaway](/wiki/Stowaway_(Fringe) "Stowaway (Fringe)")" as a special agent stationed at the FBI building in [Hartford, Connecticut](/wiki/Hartford,_Connecticut "Hartford, Connecticut"), later joining Fringe division within season 4.[[36]](#cite_note-nypost_whatif-36)[[37]](#cite_note-Holbrook-37)

### Recurring

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Fringe_(TV_series)&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Recurring")]

* [Michael Cerveris](/wiki/Michael_Cerveris "Michael Cerveris") as [September/the Observer/Donald](/wiki/The_Observer_(Fringe) "The Observer (Fringe)"): one of several "Observers," a traveling chronicler and enforcer of extraordinary events. An Observer appears in one form or another, usually in an [Alfred Hitchcock-like cameo](/wiki/List_of_cameo_appearances_by_Alfred_Hitchcock "List of cameo appearances by Alfred Hitchcock"), in each episode.
* [Clark Middleton](/wiki/Clark_Middleton "Clark Middleton") as [Edward Markham](/wiki/List_of_Fringe_characters#Edward_Markham "List of Fringe characters"): A man who operates a bookstore specializing in rare collectables. He appears in one episode every season.

He reappears in season 4 after the timeline is altered.

In the alternate timeline Peter is projected into, it appears he is alive, and that the shapeshifters have been working for him.

## Development

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Fringe_(TV_series)&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Development")]

### Conception

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Fringe_(TV_series)&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Conception")]

Co-created by [J. J. Abrams](/wiki/J._J._Abrams "J. J. Abrams"), [Roberto Orci](/wiki/Roberto_Orci "Roberto Orci") and [Alex Kurtzman](/wiki/Alex_Kurtzman "Alex Kurtzman"), *Fringe* was produced by [Bad Robot](/wiki/Bad_Robot "Bad Robot") in association with [Warner Bros. Television](/wiki/Warner_Bros._Television "Warner Bros. Television"), as part of a commitment that Abrams previously made with the studio.[[38]](#cite_note-nytimes_creation-38) At the time, Abrams was working with Orci and Kurtzman on the *[Star Trek](/wiki/Star_Trek_(2009_film) "Star Trek (2009 film)")* film, and met at one of the [Comic-Cons](/wiki/San_Diego_Comic-Con_International "San Diego Comic-Con International") during *Star Trek*'s production to brainstorm ideas for the show.[[38]](#cite_note-nytimes_creation-38)[[39]](#cite_note-tvguide_oralhistory_pt1-39) Abrams later brought [Bryan Burk](/wiki/Bryan_Burk "Bryan Burk"), a producer on several of his films, to help with developing the series.[[39]](#cite_note-tvguide_oralhistory_pt1-39)

Abrams's inspiration for *Fringe* came from a range of sources, including the writings of [Michael Crichton](/wiki/Michael_Crichton "Michael Crichton") and [Robin Cook](/wiki/Robin_Cook_(American_novelist) "Robin Cook (American novelist)"), the film *[Altered States](/wiki/Altered_States "Altered States")*, films by [David Cronenberg](/wiki/David_Cronenberg "David Cronenberg"), and the television series *[The Night Stalker](/wiki/Kolchak:_The_Night_Stalker "Kolchak: The Night Stalker")*, *[The X-Files](/wiki/The_X-Files "The X-Files")* and *[The Twilight Zone](/wiki/The_Twilight_Zone "The Twilight Zone")*.[[38]](#cite_note-nytimes_creation-38)[[40]](#cite_note-40) Orci stated that *Fringe* is a "new kind of storytelling", combining [procedural](/wiki/Procedural_(genre) "Procedural (genre)") shows such as *[Law & Order](/wiki/Law_%26_Order "Law & Order")*, and an "extremely serialized and very culty" series like *[Lost](/wiki/Lost_(2004_TV_series) "Lost (2004 TV series)")*.[[41]](#cite_note-sfw_interview-41) The procedural aspect was chosen because, at the time of its premiere, six of the ten top shows were procedural in nature; Orci stated that "you have to be a fool not to go study what it is that they're doing".[[38]](#cite_note-nytimes_creation-38) Abrams had originally considered naming the series *The Lab*, as they had envisioned Walter's laboratory to be "the epicenter of the conversation", and where "anything is possible".[[39]](#cite_note-tvguide_oralhistory_pt1-39)

Though the team saw this as a way of presenting "mystery of the week"-type episodes, they wanted to focus more on how these stories were told in unpredictable ways rather than the actual mystery, recognizing that most of their target audience has seen such mysteries before through previous shows and films.[[41]](#cite_note-sfw_interview-41) Instead, they wanted their storytelling to be original and unexpected, and, as claimed by Kurtzman, one of the most challenging aspects of developing the individual episodes.[[41]](#cite_note-sfw_interview-41) Serialization of the show was important to tell their overall story with larger plot elements, but Abrams recognized the difficulties that his earlier serialized shows, such as *Lost* and *[Alias](/wiki/Alias_(TV_series) "Alias (TV series)")*, had in attracting and maintaining viewers that had not seen these shows from the start or who missed episodes sporadically.[[38]](#cite_note-nytimes_creation-38) For *Fringe*, Abrams instead sought to create, as stated by David Itzkoff of the *[New York Times](/wiki/New_York_Times "New York Times")*, "a show that suggested complexity but was comprehensible in any given episode".[[38]](#cite_note-nytimes_creation-38)[[39]](#cite_note-tvguide_oralhistory_pt1-39) The writers aimed to balance a line between stand-alone episodes, a factor requested by Fox, and a heavily serialized show; they balanced these by moving the serialization aspect to the growth and development of their characters. This gave them the ability to write self-contained episodes that still contained elements related to the overall mythos.[[39]](#cite_note-tvguide_oralhistory_pt1-39) However, as production continued, the creative staff found the show itself took on a more serialized nature and opted towards this approach in later seasons while still balancing self-contained episodes.[[39]](#cite_note-tvguide_oralhistory_pt1-39)

One method was by introducing overarching themes that individual episodes could be tied to, such as "The Pattern" in Season 1, providing information repeatedly about the larger plot over the course of several episodes or seasons.[[38]](#cite_note-nytimes_creation-38) Abrams also created characters whose alliances to the larger narrative were clear, avoiding a similar problem that had occurred during the first and second seasons of *Alias*.[[38]](#cite_note-nytimes_creation-38) A final step taken was to script out all of the major long-running plot elements, including the show's finale, prior to full-time production. Abrams contrasted this to the process used in *Lost*, where ideas like character flashbacks and the hatch from the second season were introduced haphazardly and made difficulties in defining when they should be presented to the viewers. Instead, with *Fringe*, they were able to create "clearly defined goalposts" (in Itzkoff's words) that could be altered as necessary with network and seasonal changes but always provided a clear target for the overarching plot.[[38]](#cite_note-nytimes_creation-38) These approaches also allowed the team to introduce unique plot elements to be introduced in time that would have altered the show's fate if known at the start. Abrams stated that "There are certain details that are hugely important that I believe, if shared, will destroy any chance of actually getting on the air."[[38]](#cite_note-nytimes_creation-38) Abrams noted that they are able to benefit from "how open Fox as a network has been to a show that is embracing the weirdness and the long-term stories that we want to tell".[[41]](#cite_note-sfw_interview-41) During the third season, executive producer Jeff Pinkner noted that "We have six to eight seasons worth of material. We see it as having certain chapters that would enrich the overall story, but aren't necessary to tell the overall story. God willing, the network allows us the time to tell our complete story."[[42]](#cite_note-42)

As part of the larger story, the writers have placed elements in earlier episodes that are referenced in episodes seasons later. For example, in the first-season episode "[The Ghost Network](/wiki/The_Ghost_Network "The Ghost Network")", the Fringe team encounters an amber-like substance, which is later shown to be a critical means to combat the breakdown of the parallel universe and eventually for the same in the prime universe with the third-season episode "[6B](/wiki/6B_(Fringe) "6B (Fringe)")".[[24]](#cite_note-ign_march2011_part2-24) Pinkner compared this aspect to "planting seeds", some which they know how they plan to use later in the show's story, while others they can find ways of incorporating into these later episodes.[[24]](#cite_note-ign_march2011_part2-24)

He further attributed these elements as part of the "world building" to flesh out the show beyond episodic content.[[24]](#cite_note-ign_march2011_part2-24) The producers have stated that when the show's mythology is introduced, it is not simply there to tie episodes together, but "to provide answers that generate consequences".[[43]](#cite_note-43)

Certain elements of the show's mythology were established from the start. The parallel universe was always part of the original concept, though aspects of when and how to introduce it were tackled as the show proceeded.[[44]](#cite_note-tvguide_oralhistory_pt2-44) The idea of Peter being from the parallel universe came early into the show's production, but this came to lead the team to jokingly refer to Peter as their "hatch", one of the early mysteries in *Lost*, as with the hatch in *Lost*, they initially had no idea how to introduce this within the show.[[44]](#cite_note-tvguide_oralhistory_pt2-44) FOX's Reilly was also initially concerned about the parallel universe aspect, but as the show progressed into the first season and found its groove, the concept was readily accepted.[[44]](#cite_note-tvguide_oralhistory_pt2-44) The most poignant introduction of the parallel universe was in the conclusion of the first-season finale, "[There's More Than One of Everything](/wiki/There%27s_More_Than_One_of_Everything "There's More Than One of Everything")", where they showed the Twin Towers of the [World Trade Center](/wiki/World_Trade_Center_(1973%E2%80%932001) "World Trade Center (1973–2001)") were still standing in the parallel universe, a concept introduced by writer [Andrew Kreisberg](/wiki/Andrew_Kreisberg "Andrew Kreisberg") as an iconic image to leave viewers with; Jackson stated, "I don't know that we've ever had a better visual or a better cliff-hanger."[[44]](#cite_note-tvguide_oralhistory_pt2-44)

Other mythos elements were devised as the series progressed. The writers had originally envisioned only spending small portions of episodes within the parallel universe, but as they wrote these episodes within Season 3, they brought out the idea of setting entire episodes within the parallel universe.[[44]](#cite_note-tvguide_oralhistory_pt2-44) This necessitated the development of the alternate versions of the main characters, which Pinkner considered "a great playground just for imagination".[[44]](#cite_note-tvguide_oralhistory_pt2-44) The actors themselves found this concept exciting, as it allowed them to play different characters but with the same background and considered it a creative challenge.[[44]](#cite_note-tvguide_oralhistory_pt2-44) Similarly, having Peter erased from the timeline as part of the finale for Season 3 was an idea that grew over the course of that season.[[45]](#cite_note-tvguide_oralhistory_pt3-45) The writers were aware this would be a risky move but felt the idea was very appropriate for *Fringe*, and opted to write towards this after considering all the consequences.[[45]](#cite_note-tvguide_oralhistory_pt3-45) Pinkner noted that this gave them an opportunity to "reset the character relationships" and determine the key aspects that would remain without Peter, as well as "making the audience uncomfortable at times".[[45]](#cite_note-tvguide_oralhistory_pt3-45) The cast was not initially sold with the idea, but came around as the fourth season progressed.[[45]](#cite_note-tvguide_oralhistory_pt3-45)

### Production

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Fringe_(TV_series)&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Production")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5d/Jeff_Pinkner_by_Gage_Skidmore_2.jpg/250px-Jeff_Pinkner_by_Gage_Skidmore_2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Jeff_Pinkner_by_Gage_Skidmore_2.jpg)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e0/J._H._Wyman_by_Gage_Skidmore_2.jpg/250px-J._H._Wyman_by_Gage_Skidmore_2.jpg)](/wiki/File:J._H._Wyman_by_Gage_Skidmore_2.jpg)

After developing the core concepts of the show, Abrams began to seek studios to develop the show; Abrams' past successes led to Warner Bros. Television and the Fox Television Network to quickly jump on board the project. [Peter Roth](/wiki/Peter_Roth_(executive) "Peter Roth (executive)"), the chief executive of Warner Bros. Television, had been actively seeking to bring any Abrams' project to his studio, and heard Abrams' pitch for *Fringe* at a dinner meeting in August 2007.[[39]](#cite_note-tvguide_oralhistory_pt1-39) Similarly, [Kevin Reilly](/wiki/Kevin_Reilly_(executive) "Kevin Reilly (executive)"), the entertainment chairman at Fox, knew that it was critical for him to bring the next Abrams' project to his network, and worked with Roth to assure this would happen.[[39]](#cite_note-tvguide_oralhistory_pt1-39)

[Jeff Pinkner](/wiki/Jeff_Pinkner "Jeff Pinkner") was selected to act as the head [showrunner](/wiki/Showrunner "Showrunner") and [executive producer](/wiki/Executive_producer "Executive producer"). Abrams noted that he trusts Pinkner after working together with him on *[Alias](/wiki/Alias_(TV_series) "Alias (TV series)")* and *Lost*.[[46]](#cite_note-Jeff_Pinkner_Showrunner-46) Pinkner became interested in the show during a visit to the set of *Star Trek*, during which Abrams was discussing the concept of *Fringe* with Orci and Kurtzman, knowing that they would not be involved in the direct production of the work. Abrams pitched the idea to Pinkner, who was intrigued by the importance of characters in a science fiction drama.[[39]](#cite_note-tvguide_oralhistory_pt1-39) In season two, J.H. Wyman was brought on as executive producer and showrunner with Pinkner. Wyman had been a science fiction enthusiast but had worried that he had not written anything in that genre but after learning about the concept of the show, felt his role as an executive producer was "a match made in heaven".[[39]](#cite_note-tvguide_oralhistory_pt1-39)

[Michael Giacchino](/wiki/Michael_Giacchino "Michael Giacchino"), Abrams' frequent collaborator, composed the music for the pilot of *Fringe*, before handing over duties to his assistants Chad Seiter and [Chris Tilton](/wiki/Chris_Tilton "Chris Tilton"); Tilton took over scoring duties from Season 2 onward.[[47]](#cite_note-47) While Giacchino retains an on-screen credit, Abrams himself wrote the series theme music.[[48]](#cite_note-48)

The two-hour pilot episode, filmed in Toronto, Ontario, Canada, cost a total of $10 million to create.[[49]](#cite_note-49) A basement of an old church was used for Walter's lab set in the pilot, and this set was replicated at other film sites in New York and Vancouver when the show moved.[[50]](#cite_note-latimesnoblehanson-50) The lab set was designed by [Carol Spier](/wiki/Carol_Spier "Carol Spier"), the production designer for several of Cronenberg's films.[[39]](#cite_note-tvguide_oralhistory_pt1-39) John Noble called his character's lab "the heart and soul of *Fringe*", so consequently, "That has to remain constant."[[50]](#cite_note-latimesnoblehanson-50) A cow used in the pilot episode had to be recast when production of Season 1 was moved to New York, due to livestock restrictions preventing her from being brought from Canada to the United States.[[51]](#cite_note-51)[[52]](#cite_note-52) Other locations used in the first season included other universities to stage for the [Harvard University](/wiki/Harvard_University "Harvard University") campus, where Walter's lab is located. These included [Pratt Institute](/wiki/Pratt_Institute "Pratt Institute") and [Yale University](/wiki/Yale_University "Yale University"),[[53]](#cite_note-paradeharvardsites-53) including its [Old Campus](/wiki/Old_Campus "Old Campus") (particularly Phelps Hall and Durfee Hall), [Branford College](/wiki/Branford_College "Branford College"), and the exterior of [Yale Law School](/wiki/Yale_Law_School "Yale Law School"), [University of Toronto](/wiki/University_of_Toronto "University of Toronto")'s [University College](/wiki/University_College,_University_of_Toronto "University College, University of Toronto"), [Brooklyn College](/wiki/Brooklyn_College "Brooklyn College").[[53]](#cite_note-paradeharvardsites-53)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/61/Fringe_filming_in_vancouver.jpg/250px-Fringe_filming_in_vancouver.jpg)](/wiki/File:Fringe_filming_in_vancouver.jpg)

Filming of the *Fringe* episode "[6B](/wiki/6B_(Fringe) "6B (Fringe)")" in December 2010 at the Vancouver Film School building at Hastings and Cambie streets in Vancouver, staged as New York City

On February 21, 2009, it was reported that in the event that *Fringe* would be renewed for a second season, the show would move production to [Vancouver](/wiki/Vancouver "Vancouver") from [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City") as a cost-cutting measure.[[54]](#cite_note-54) Executive producer Jeff Pinkner explained:

> We want to stay in New York, New York has been incredibly good to us. It feels like we're being kicked out of the city. I know we're not, but they're making it impossible for us to afford doing the show ... Our New York crew is spectacular, they've worked their [butts] off to make the show look great. But it looks like New York is not renewing a tax credit that makes it possible to make our budget in New York. So it looks like, out of necessity, we'll have to leave New York, which is not anything we are welcoming.[[55]](#cite_note-chicago_tribune-55)

As plans were being made to move the production to Canada, the New York state legislature passed continuation of the film and tax credits, as planned.[[56]](#cite_note-56) Upon productions moving to Vancouver, British Columbia, Canada for season 2, the [University of British Columbia](/wiki/University_of_British_Columbia "University of British Columbia") now stands in for Harvard.[[53]](#cite_note-paradeharvardsites-53) The area around [New Westminster](/wiki/New_Westminster "New Westminster") often serves as filming locations for *Fringe* stories that take place in the parallel universe.[[57]](#cite_note-57)[[58]](#cite_note-58)

Prior to the start of production for the fifth season, Pinkner announced that he was leaving the production of the show to pursue other projects; Wyman would remain as the sole showrunner for the show.[[59]](#cite_note-59)

### Casting

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Fringe_(TV_series)&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Casting")]

The show's main characters, Olivia, Peter, and Walter, were at the core of the concept for *Fringe*. The creators recognized early that "the idea that telling a father-son story and a relationship story was a really compelling one and a very accessible one", according to Kurtzman.[[41]](#cite_note-sfw_interview-41) They were able to provide the characters with backstories that, like with other long-term plot elements, could be alluded to over several episodes and seasons.[[41]](#cite_note-sfw_interview-41)

The characters would also contrast with the typical procedural genre show; rather than having clearly defined roles episode to episode, they instead "have an emotional memory and an emotional investment", as stated by Orci.[[41]](#cite_note-sfw_interview-41) This also allowed for, as necessary, characters to be removed or introduced to the show and have a larger impact on the other characters.[[41]](#cite_note-sfw_interview-41)

The first actors cast were [Kirk Acevedo](/wiki/Kirk_Acevedo "Kirk Acevedo") and [Mark Valley](/wiki/Mark_Valley "Mark Valley"), who portrayed FBI agents Charlie Francis and John Scott, respectively.[[60]](#cite_note-60)[[61]](#cite_note-61) [John Noble](/wiki/John_Noble "John Noble") and [Lance Reddick](/wiki/Lance_Reddick "Lance Reddick"), who play Dr. Walter Bishop and [Homeland Security](/wiki/Homeland_Security "Homeland Security") agent Phillip Broyles, joined the cast later on.[[62]](#cite_note-62)[[63]](#cite_note-63) Casting of [Anna Torv](/wiki/Anna_Torv "Anna Torv"), [Blair Brown](/wiki/Blair_Brown "Blair Brown"), and [Jasika Nicole](/wiki/Jasika_Nicole "Jasika Nicole"), who play Olivia Dunham, Massive Dynamic employee Nina Sharp, and Astrid Farnsworth, a federal agent and assistant to Olivia Dunham, respectively, followed;[[64]](#cite_note-64) while [Joshua Jackson](/wiki/Joshua_Jackson "Joshua Jackson"), who plays Peter Bishop, was the last main character to be cast.[[65]](#cite_note-65) Jackson auditioned for [James T. Kirk](/wiki/James_T._Kirk "James T. Kirk") in Abrams' *[Star Trek](/wiki/Star_Trek_(2009_film) "Star Trek (2009 film)")* and believed this is what impressed the producer to cast him in his television project,[[66]](#cite_note-66) though Abrams later clarified that it was recalling his previous experience with working with Jackson on his television series *[Felicity](/wiki/Felicity_(TV_series) "Felicity (TV series)")*.[[39]](#cite_note-tvguide_oralhistory_pt1-39) For the principal and recurring actors, they were all intrigued by the script for the pilot, comparing it to a two-hour-long movie, as well as Abrams' reputation.[[39]](#cite_note-tvguide_oralhistory_pt1-39)

On April 8, 2009, it was announced that [Leonard Nimoy](/wiki/Leonard_Nimoy "Leonard Nimoy") would appear as Walter Bishop's former lab partner, Dr. William Bell, in the first season's finale, which explores the existence of an ominous [parallel universe](/wiki/Parallel_universe_(fiction) "Parallel universe (fiction)"). This choice led one reviewer to question if *Fringe*'s plot might be a [homage](/wiki/Homage_(arts) "Homage (arts)") to the *[Star Trek](/wiki/Star_Trek:_The_Original_Series "Star Trek: The Original Series")* episode "[Mirror, Mirror](/wiki/Mirror,_Mirror_(Star_Trek) "Mirror, Mirror (Star Trek)")", which explored the concept of an alternate reality referred to as a "[mirror universe](/wiki/Mirror_universe "Mirror universe")", and an evil version of [Spock](/wiki/Spock "Spock") distinguished by a goatee.[[67]](#cite_note-67) Nimoy returned as Dr. Bell for an extended arc, and according to Orci, Bell is "the beginning of the answers to even bigger questions."[[68]](#cite_note-68)[[69]](#cite_note-69) Nimoy reprised his role in the second-season finale, where his character and Walter met for a "showdown".[[70]](#cite_note-showdown-70) Nimoy's character is apparently dead after the season finale, having used himself to help Walter, Peter and the Alternate Olivia back to the prime universe. As he had retired from acting, it was thought unlikely that his character would return.[[71]](#cite_note-71) In February 2011 however, he announced his definite plan to return to *Fringe* and reprise his role as William Bell.[[72]](#cite_note-72) He returned to voice the character in the animated segments of the third-season episode "[Lysergic Acid Diethylamide](/wiki/Lysergic_Acid_Diethylamide_(Fringe) "Lysergic Acid Diethylamide (Fringe)")",[[73]](#cite_note-73) and appeared as a computer-generated character in the fourth-season episode "[Letters of Transit](/wiki/Letters_of_Transit "Letters of Transit")".[[74]](#cite_note-74) In the two-part finale of the fourth season, Nimoy returned to play the ultimate antagonist of the alternate timeline story arc.

## Reception

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Fringe_(TV_series)&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Reception")]

Early reception through the first season was generally lukewarm, with many noting its "lackluster" start and the "dullness" of the characters. However, changes in the approach and storytelling of the show in the second and subsequent seasons led to more positive critical reception and made it a media favorite. As a whole, the series was well received by the critics and has developed a [cult following](/wiki/Cult_following "Cult following").[[75]](#cite_note-avclub_2016-75)

### First season

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Fringe_(TV_series)&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: First season")]

The pilot episode was watched by 9.13 million viewers, garnering 3.2/9 [Nielsen ratings](/wiki/Nielsen_ratings "Nielsen ratings") among adults 18–49, with ratings improving over the course of the episode.[[76]](#cite_note-76) Ratings improved greatly for the second episode, "[The Same Old Story](/wiki/The_Same_Old_Story "The Same Old Story")" which 13.27 million people watched, making it the fifth most watched show of the week.[[77]](#cite_note-77) As of October 2008, the show had achieved the first place in the 18–49 [demographic](/wiki/Demographic_profile "Demographic profile") among new shows.[[78]](#cite_note-78) Barry Garron at *[The Hollywood Reporter](/wiki/The_Hollywood_Reporter "The Hollywood Reporter")* found it promising and "reminiscent of battle-of-the-sexes charm."[[79]](#cite_note-79) Robert Bianco, of *[USA Today](/wiki/USA_Today "USA Today")*, said, "What Abrams brings to Fringe is a director's eye for plot and pace, a fan's love of sci-fi excitement, and a story-teller's gift for investing absurd events with real emotions and relatable characters."[[80]](#cite_note-80) Travis Fickett of [IGN](/wiki/IGN "IGN") gave it 7.6 out of 10, calling it "a lackluster pilot that promises to be a pretty good series".[[81]](#cite_note-81) While Tim Goodman of the *[San Francisco Chronicle](/wiki/San_Francisco_Chronicle "San Francisco Chronicle")* remarked that it was "boundlessly ambitious",[[82]](#cite_note-82) *[Chicago Sun-Times](/wiki/Chicago_Sun-Times "Chicago Sun-Times")*'s Misha Davenport called it an "update of *[The X-Files](/wiki/The_X-Files "The X-Files")* with the addition of terrorism and the office of Homeland Security".[[83]](#cite_note-83) In a 2016 retrospective of the series, *A.V. Club* writer Joshua Alston noted that the similarities to *The X-Files*, including the "monster of the week" approach and tepid romance between two of the series' lead characters, as well as the relatively recent release of *[The X-Files: I Want to Believe](/wiki/The_X-Files:_I_Want_to_Believe "The X-Files: I Want to Believe")*, had turned audiences off the show since "it was retreading what *The X-Files* had already done."[[75]](#cite_note-avclub_2016-75)

In its *2008 Year in Review*, [Television Without Pity](/wiki/Television_Without_Pity "Television Without Pity") declared *Fringe* one of the year's biggest TV disappointments, commenting that the show is "entertaining" and "the cast is largely strong" but the character development is insufficient, and describing main character Olivia Dunham as "wooden and distant."[[84]](#cite_note-TV_W/O_Pity-84) Meanwhile, in other articles recounting the best and worst of 2008, *[The New York Times](/wiki/The_New_York_Times "The New York Times")* stated that *Fringe* "is the best of a rash of new series that toy with the paranormal". The author goes on to praise the cast saying that "Much credit belongs to Anna Torv who stars as an F.B.I. agent investigating bizarre murders that all appear to be linked to a powerful and mysterious multinational corporation", and "Ms. Torv is backed up ably by John Noble as a crazy but brilliant fringe scientist and his level-headed but skeptical son, played by Joshua Jackson".[[85]](#cite_note-85)

The first season holds an 85% approval rating on review aggregator [Rotten Tomatoes](/wiki/Rotten_Tomatoes "Rotten Tomatoes"), based on 26 critic reviews. The website's critics consensus reads, "Action-packed, suspenseful, and filled with intriguing twists, *Fringe* is a smart sci-fi series that's compelling enough to overcome its occasionally uneven plotting."[[86]](#cite_note-86)

### Subsequent seasons

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Fringe_(TV_series)&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Subsequent seasons")]

Seasons of *Fringe* after the first season were seen as vast improvements on the first.[[75]](#cite_note-avclub_2016-75) *[Entertainment Weekly](/wiki/Entertainment_Weekly "Entertainment Weekly")* stated, "The best new show of the year took a few weeks to grow on me, but now it's a full-blown addiction."[[87]](#cite_note-87) The *[Los Angeles Times](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Times "Los Angeles Times")* called Walter Bishop one of the best characters of 2008, noting, "the role of the modern-day mad scientist could so easily have been a disaster, but the 'Fringe' writers and the masterful John Noble have conspired to create a character that seems, as trite as it sounds, more Shakespearean than sci-fi."[[88]](#cite_note-88)

*[Chicago Tribune](/wiki/Chicago_Tribune "Chicago Tribune")* stated that some episodes are "distressingly predictable and formulaic" but adds that there have also been some excellent episodes.[[55]](#cite_note-chicago_tribune-55) The *New York Times* named *Fringe* one of the top 10 television shows in 2010,[[89]](#cite_note-89) while Television Without Pity, previously dismissive of the show, listed it amongst their 2010 "Most Memorable TV Moments", stating "there were so many great *Fringe* moments this year" and "we were treated to some of the best sci-fi on television this past fall".[[90]](#cite_note-90)

*[The A.V. Club](/wiki/The_A.V._Club "The A.V. Club")* named *Fringe* the 15th best show of 2010, stated that the episode "Peter" gave "the series' overarching storyline a devastating emotional core", making the series a "rare blend of inventive ideas, wild ambition, and unexpected soulfulness".[[91]](#cite_note-91) [IGN](/wiki/IGN "IGN") named *Fringe* the 18th best science fiction show of all time in a 2011 listing, stating that since the middle of the first season, "it's been nothing but a series of satisfyingly jaw-dropping 'holy eff!' moments layered with wonderful, nuanced performances from Anna Torv, Joshua Jackson and John Noble".[[92]](#cite_note-92)

In 2012, *Entertainment Weekly* listed the show at No. 17 in the "25 Best Cult TV Shows from the Past 25 Years", saying, "*Fringe* was conceived as a mass-appeal genre procedural, with a background mythology that wouldn't detract from [monster-of-the-week](/wiki/Villain_of_the_week "Villain of the week") episodes. ... But the mythology overtook the monsters following the revelation of a [parallel universe](/wiki/Parallel_universe_(fiction) "Parallel universe (fiction)"). By its third season, *Fringe* was overpopulated by multiple versions of every character. Unfortunately, that increasing narrative complexity has steadily pigeonholed it as a niche show."[[93]](#cite_note-93)

In 2015, *[Bustle](/wiki/Bustle_(magazine) "Bustle (magazine)")* declared "[White Tulip](/wiki/White_Tulip "White Tulip")" as "one of the greatest hours in television history". He wrote, "'White Tulip' is an hour of television that shouldn't work, that technically doesn't even exist ... but thank god it does, because it's one of the most powerful episodes ever created — and it showcases why *Fringe* is one of the most criminally underrated series of our time."[[94]](#cite_note-94)

On review aggregator Rotten Tomatoes, the second season holds an 82% approval rating based on 17 critic reviews. The website's critics consensus reads, "*Fringe* surpasses expectations in season two with stronger character development while maintaining its creepy sci-fi angle."[[95]](#cite_note-95) The third season holds a 100% approval rating based on 14 reviews, with the critics consensus stating, "With more mysteries to uncover and mind-bending plot devices, season three of *Fringe* further cements the show's status as one of the ... best science fiction shows on television."[[96]](#cite_note-96) The fourth season holds a 100% approval rating based on 13 reviews, with the critics consensus stating, "A daring reset may baffle casual viewers, but those already enthralled by Fringe's knotty mythology will likely become even more invested." [[97]](#cite_note-97) The fifth and final season holds an 88% approval rating based on 17 reviews. The critics consensus reads, "*Fringe* overcomes a compressed episode count and humbled production values to deliver a moving and rousing conclusion to its fans."[[98]](#cite_note-98)

*Fringe* premiered in the [2008 United States television season](/wiki/2008%E2%80%9309_United_States_network_television_schedule "2008–09 United States network television schedule") at a regular timeslot of 9:00 p.m. Eastern on Tuesdays. During Season 1, *Fringe* was part of a Fox initiative known as "Remote-Free TV". Episodes of *Fringe* were longer than standard dramas on current network television. The show ran with half the commercials, adding about six minutes to the show's runtime.[[114]](#cite_note-Remote_Free-114) When the show went to a commercial, a short bumper aired informing the viewer of roughly how much time commercials would consume before the program resumed. The pilot episode was leaked via [BitTorrent](/wiki/BitTorrent "BitTorrent"), three months before the series premiere; similar to leaked fellow Fox series *[Terminator: The Sarah Connor Chronicles](/wiki/Terminator:_The_Sarah_Connor_Chronicles "Terminator: The Sarah Connor Chronicles")*.[[115]](#cite_note-115) The series was renewed for a second season on May 4, 2009,[[116]](#cite_note-116) and moved to 9:00 p.m. on Thursday.[[117]](#cite_note-117)

It was renewed for a third season on March 6, 2010.[[118]](#cite_note-118) As part of a reorganization of its [2010 midseason](/wiki/2010%E2%80%932011_United_States_network_television_schedule "2010–2011 United States network television schedule") line up to capture more market for *[American Idol](/wiki/American_Idol "American Idol")*, the Fox network shifted *Fringe* to 9:00 p.m. on Fridays. This timeslot, commonly considered the "[Friday night death slot](/wiki/Friday_night_death_slot "Friday night death slot")" for several previous Fox shows due to cancellation shortly following the move to that slot, left critics considering the show's fate. While *The X-Files* originally premiered during this slot and would continue to be a highly successful series, critics were unsure if *Fringe* could duplicate this performance. In this slot, the show competed with *[Supernatural](/wiki/Supernatural_(American_TV_series) "Supernatural (American TV series)")*, a series that attracts similar types of viewers.[[119]](#cite_note-119) Fox's Entertainment President [Kevin Reilly](/wiki/Kevin_Reilly_(executive) "Kevin Reilly (executive)"), in response to these concerns, stated that 45% of *Fringe*'s viewership is from [time shifting](/wiki/Time_shifting "Time shifting") recording through digital video receivers, and does not expect the viewership numbers to change significantly with the change to Friday. Reilly further postulated that "If it does anywhere near what it did on Thursdays, we can glue that show to the schedule because it can be a big win for us".[[120]](#cite_note-120) Further promoted by the critical reaction to the rescheduling, the Fox network created a self-deprecating promotional advertisement acknowledging the reputation of the time slot, including quotes from other media outlets concerned about the move, but asserted that the move would "re-animate" the show.[[121]](#cite_note-121)[[122]](#cite_note-122) The network also created a music video, set to "[Echoes](/wiki/Echoes_(Klaxons_song) "Echoes (Klaxons song)")" by the band [Klaxons](/wiki/Klaxons "Klaxons") as a means of summarizing the third season to date prior to the first Friday broadcast.[[123]](#cite_note-123) Joshua Jackson, who plays Peter Bishop on the show, cautioned that time-shifted viewership may not be enough to save the show: "It's not that not enough people are watching *Fringe*, it's that not enough people are watching *Fringe* during the hour that it's on the air, which is key for the network."[[124]](#cite_note-124)

Producers Pinkner and Wyman also were excited about the move to Fridays, considering the slot as "open territory that can be conquered" and that they "can actually deliver like *The X-Files* did".[[125]](#cite_note-125) Series creator Abrams was less optimistic of the move to Friday nights, aware that the show's likelihood to be renewed for a fourth season would be highly dependent on the number of niche viewers that continue to watch the show.[[126]](#cite_note-abrams_fridaynight-126) Abrams did affirm that moving to Friday nights allows them to take more creative freedoms to maintain viewership in the new timeslot, but feels that if the show was not renewed for another season, they would be "hard pressed" to resolve the story by the end of the third season.[[126]](#cite_note-abrams_fridaynight-126)

The show's first episode at the Friday 9:00 p.m. timeslot ("[The Firefly](/wiki/The_Firefly_(Fringe) "The Firefly (Fringe)")") scored a 1.9 in the key 18–49 demographic which was an increase of 12% over its last Thursday-aired episode ("Marionette") which scored a 1.7,[[127]](#cite_note-127) and maintained similar numbers in the second week for "[Reciprocity](/wiki/Reciprocity_(Fringe) "Reciprocity (Fringe)")".[[128]](#cite_note-128) Though viewership slipped in further weeks, the show was renewed for a fourth season in March 2011. The move was unexpected based on these ratings, given the past performance of shows with similar viewership numbers in the Friday night slot, but several critics attribute it to the strong fanbase that the show has garnered, which contributes in part to consistently higher time-shifted viewership.[[129]](#cite_note-129) Fox's Reilly stated that:

> *Fringe* has truly hit a creative stride and has distinguished itself as one of television's most original programs. The series' ingenious producers, amazingly talented cast and crew, as well as some of the most passionate and loyal fans on the planet, made this fourth-season pickup possible. When we moved the show to Fridays, we asked the fans to follow and they did. We're thrilled to bring it back for another full season and keep it part of the Fox family.

— Kevin Reilly, President of Fox Entertainment[[130]](#cite_note-130)

Reilly further added that the renewal was also prompted by the high risk of trying to replace *Fringe* with another show with unknown viewership metrics; he commented that they "have a far better shot of sticking with a show that has an audience that [they] think [they] can grow".[[131]](#cite_note-131) Prior to the onset of the fourth season, Reilly reiterated that they do not expect any significant growth in *Fringe*'s viewership within that season: "It's a pretty complex show. If *Fringe* can do exactly what *Fringe* did last year, we're going to be very happy. They're right in the pocket creatively once again."[[132]](#cite_note-132) Wyman stated in an interview with *[The Hollywood Reporter](/wiki/The_Hollywood_Reporter "The Hollywood Reporter")* that the Fox network had been "supportive throughout this process", and though the show's viewership "wasn't exactly what they would've hoped for", aspects such as a loyal fan base and supportive critics were enough to take the show forward for another season.[[133]](#cite_note-133) Pinkner commented that "there were no creative conditions" on the show's renewal, nor any cuts in the show's budget, though was unsure if the show will be moved to a different time slot.[[134]](#cite_note-134) Noble, at the 2011 Comic-Con *Fringe* panel, reiterated that the fans were responsible for the livelihood of the show, stated, "Seriously, without your efforts, your rabid support, we wouldn't be here right now. You are the best fans that ever existed".[[135]](#cite_note-ew_2011sdcc-135) The fourth season premiered on September 23, 2011.[[136]](#cite_note-136)

Before the renewal of the fifth season, Abrams stated some skepticism for a renewal opportunity, but hoped that if it was cancelled, that it may be picked up by another network. On the other hand, Abrams considered that if Fox did pick up the show for a fifth season, "the next year would be the great ending for the show".[[137]](#cite_note-ign_5th_season-137) Similarly, Fox's Reilly remained cautious about a renewal, noting that while the show is one of the top shows in the Friday night slot, "it's an expensive show. We lose a lot of money on the show."[[137]](#cite_note-ign_5th_season-137) Reilly further reiterated the dedication of the fans of the show for helping it to its fourth season and continued success.[[137]](#cite_note-ign_5th_season-137) When asked about whether the writers would have enough notice to write the fourth-season finale as a series finale if necessary, Reilly stated, "That's a [Peter Roth](/wiki/Peter_Roth_(executive) "Peter Roth (executive)") issue" (chief executive of [Warner Bros. Television](/wiki/Warner_Bros._Television "Warner Bros. Television")).[[138]](#cite_note-138) To which Roth responded, "We have no plans to give viewers closure this season because we expect the series to continue".[[139]](#cite_note-139) In late January 2012, Fox and Warner Bros. Television were working on negotiating a lower licensing cost for the show to allow a fifth season to occur; this would not only help Fox reduce its losses on the show, but would also bring the total number of episodes above 100, giving Warner Bros. a better opportunity to syndicate reruns of the show.[[140]](#cite_note-140) Before the announcement of the fifth season, Wyman and Pinkner stated that they would have created a series finale for the end of the fourth season should the show be cancelled; Wyman stated "We want to take care of the fans. We want them to see where it would have gone, but we also know the show has been a commitment and we want everyone to be satisfied with it."[[141]](#cite_note-nydn_s5_finale-141)

The show was officially renewed for a fifth and final season for the 2012–2013 television season, consisting of a shortened set of 13 episodes, on April 26, 2012, with its premiere on September 28, 2012.[[142]](#cite_note-142)[[143]](#cite_note-143) Reilly, in announcing the renewal, stated that "*Fringe* is a remarkably creative series that has set the bar as one of television's most imaginative dramas. Bringing it back for a final 13 allows us to provide the climactic conclusion that its passionate and loyal fans deserve".[[144]](#cite_note-144) The writing team crafted an idea for a cohesive story-driven set of thirteen episodes as a means to complete the series, and to "honor the audience that had served us so well was to say a proper farewell", according to Roth.[[145]](#cite_note-tvguide_oralhistory_pt4-145) Noble called it "the season for the fans", with the story revisiting many of the elements from the show's past.[[145]](#cite_note-tvguide_oralhistory_pt4-145)

## Distribution

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Fringe_(TV_series)&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Distribution")]

### International

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Fringe_(TV_series)&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: International")]

*Fringe* premiered in Canada on [CTV](/wiki/CTV_Television_Network "CTV Television Network") simultaneous to its U.S. premiere and was the most watched program in Canada that week.[[166]](#cite_note-166) The show would fluctuate between airing on CTV and [A](/wiki/CTV_2 "CTV 2") during its first two seasons. Beginning with the third season, *Fringe* was broadcast on [City](/wiki/Citytv "Citytv") in Canada.[[167]](#cite_note-167)

A version of the show (edited for time) premiered on the [Nine Network](/wiki/Nine_Network "Nine Network") in Australia on September 17, 2008. In the season one episode "[In Which We Meet Mr. Jones](/wiki/In_Which_We_Meet_Mr._Jones "In Which We Meet Mr. Jones")", the opening scene where doctors discover a parasite on Detective Loeb's heart was cut, going straight to the opening credits.[[168]](#cite_note-168) Nine Network later dropped the show from its primetime schedule.[[169]](#cite_note-169) This was temporary as the show returned during the December to January non-ratings period.[[170]](#cite_note-170) The series later moved to Nine's digital multi-channel [GO!](/wiki/9Go!_(Australian_TV_channel) "9Go! (Australian TV channel)") where the last few seasons were played out.

The series premiered in the United Kingdom on [Sky1](/wiki/Sky1 "Sky1") on October 5, 2008.[[171]](#cite_note-171)

### Syndication

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Fringe_(TV_series)&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Syndication")]

With 13 episodes in its final season, *Fringe* has a total of 100 episodes, a critical number for syndication deals for Warner Bros., and considered part of the reason for the show's final renewal. The show premiered in syndication on the [Science Network](/wiki/Science_(TV_channel) "Science (TV channel)") on November 20, 2012.[[172]](#cite_note-172)[[173]](#cite_note-173)

### Home video releases

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Fringe_(TV_series)&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Home video releases")]

The first season of *Fringe* was released on DVD and [Blu-ray Disc](/wiki/Blu-ray_Disc "Blu-ray Disc") on September 8, 2009, in region 1.[[174]](#cite_note-174)[[175]](#cite_note-175) In addition to all the episodes that had been aired, extras include three commentary tracks, unaired scenes, gag reels and behind the scenes features. A "*Fringe* Pattern Analysis" feature is included on the Blu-ray version as an exclusive. The sets were released on September 28, 2009, in region 2[[176]](#cite_note-176) and on September 30, 2009, in region 4.[[177]](#cite_note-177)

The second season features four commentary tracks, a gag reel, deleted scenes, behind the scenes videos, and the episode "[Unearthed](/wiki/Unearthed_(Fringe) "Unearthed (Fringe)")", an episode, produced for the first season, which aired out of schedule during the second season. It was released on DVD and Blu-ray on September 14, 2010, in region 1,[[178]](#cite_note-178)[[179]](#cite_note-179) on September 27, 2010, in region 2,[[180]](#cite_note-180) and on November 10, 2010, in region 4.[[181]](#cite_note-181)

The third season features two commentary tracks, a gag reel, behind the scenes videos, and two features exclusive to the Blu-ray version. It was released on DVD and Blu-ray on September 6, 2011, in region 1,[[182]](#cite_note-182)[[183]](#cite_note-183) on September 26, 2011, in region 2,[[184]](#cite_note-184) and on October 26, 2011, in region 4.[[185]](#cite_note-185)

The fourth season includes several special features, including "The Culture of *Fringe*", a roundtable discussion with series writers and university professors regarding the science featured in the series; features on how the disappearance of Peter affects the timeline, and the role of the Observers; two features covering the *Fringe* comic series; and a gag reel.[[186]](#cite_note-186) It was released on DVD and Blu-ray on September 4, 2011, in region 1,[[187]](#cite_note-187) on September 24, 2012, in region 2,[[188]](#cite_note-188) and on October 31, 2012, in region 4.[[189]](#cite_note-189)

The fifth season includes featurettes titled "A Farewell to *Fringe*" and "*Fringe* Panel at Comic-Con 2012", as well audio commentaries and a gag reel. It was released on Blu-ray and DVD on May 7, 2013, in region 1[[190]](#cite_note-190) and on May 13, 2013, in region 2.[[191]](#cite_note-191) A complete series box set was also released on the same dates of the fifth season release in the respective regions.[[192]](#cite_note-192)[[193]](#cite_note-193)

## Other media

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Fringe_(TV_series)&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Other media")]

### Games

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Fringe_(TV_series)&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Games")]

An [alternate reality game](/wiki/Alternate_reality_game "Alternate reality game"), centered on the fictional Massive Dynamic corporation, was introduced during the pilot and featured "strange symbols paired with glowing dots" appearing throughout the episode and an "advertisement" for the company shown at the end with a web address for the game.[[194]](#cite_note-194)

### Books and comics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Fringe_(TV_series)&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Books and comics")]

On August 27, 2008, a [prequel](/wiki/Prequel "Prequel") [comic book](/wiki/Comic_book "Comic book") (leading right up to the moment in the pilot where Olivia 'first' meets Walter) written by [Zack Whedon](/wiki/Zack_Whedon "Zack Whedon") was released by [DC Comics](/wiki/DC_Comics "DC Comics") under its [WildStorm](/wiki/WildStorm "WildStorm") imprint.[[195]](#cite_note-USA_today_coming_attractions-195)[[196]](#cite_note-196) This was to be the first issue of a monthly 6-issue [limited series](/wiki/Limited_series_(comics) "Limited series (comics)") but the others were delayed until January 2009, when monthly publication resumed, with the sixth and final issue scheduled for release on June 17.[[197]](#cite_note-197) The Vice President of WildStorm, [Hank Kanalz](/wiki/Hank_Kanalz "Hank Kanalz"), explained the publication hiatus: "The writers of the show want to make sure the comic book is integrated into the mythology of the *Fringe* world, so we have decided to refocus the direction of the comic book. Unfortunately, this means that we will have some delays, but will be back in January."[[198]](#cite_note-198)

On June 23, 2010, the first issue of *Tales From the Fringe*, the second six-part monthly series, was released, while the final issue was released on November 24, 2010.[[199]](#cite_note-199)

Additionally, in September 2011, DC released the first issue of *Beyond the Fringe* comic series. With the first story written by Joshua Jackson titled "Peter and the Machine". [Jhonen Vasquez](/wiki/Jhonen_Vasquez "Jhonen Vasquez"), [Becky Cloonan](/wiki/Becky_Cloonan "Becky Cloonan"), Cole Fowler, Jorge Jimenez, Kristen Cantrell, [Ben Templesmith](/wiki/Ben_Templesmith "Ben Templesmith"), [Tom Mandrake](/wiki/Tom_Mandrake "Tom Mandrake"), and Carrie Strachen are among the other creators on the series. The comic issues will alternate with an 'A' story and a 'B' story each. For example, "Peter and the Machine" will take place in issues one, three, and so on until the story is finished and a new 'A' story starts up.[[200]](#cite_note-200)

An encyclopedia guide, *September's Notebook — The Bishop Paradox*, written by Tara Bennett and Paul Terry who had previously written the *Lost Encyclopedia* for *[Lost](/wiki/Lost_(2004_TV_series) "Lost (2004 TV series)")*, was released in March 2013.[[201]](#cite_note-201) A three-part series of prequel novels written by [Christa Faust](/wiki/Christa_Faust "Christa Faust") was released throughout 2013 and 2014. Each novel deals with a member of the Fringe team's past. The first is titled *The Zodiac Paradox* and is about Walter and his discovery of Cortexiphan. The second in the series is titled *The Burning Man* and is based on Olivia and how she was first exposed to Cortexiphan. The third is titled *Sins of the Father* and is about Peter's life in 2008 just before the point of time when the series starts.[[202]](#cite_note-202)

### Possible film

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Fringe_(TV_series)&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Possible film")]

At the 2012 [San Diego Comic-Con](/wiki/San_Diego_Comic-Con "San Diego Comic-Con"), actor [John Noble](/wiki/John_Noble "John Noble") speculated that a film could be made further down the line.[[203]](#cite_note-203)
